content.text: is the primary mechanism Python programs use to detect, classify, and recover from runtime errors. In agentic systems — where a single user request may trigger dozens of tool calls, API round-trips, and state mutations — mastering this construct is what separates agents that degrade gracefully from those that crash silently or leak resources under load.
Python's exception model is based on the idea that ordinary control-flow and error-flow should be clearly separated. The
block delimits code whose execution may raise an exception. One or more
clauses follow, each specifying the exception type (or tuple of types) to catch and the recovery code to run. An optional
body completed without raising any exception. The
clause — also optional — always runs before control leaves the entire construct, regardless of whether an exception was raised, caught, re-raised, or the function returned mid-try.
All four clauses are part of a single compound statement. The general shape is:

Agentic systems are long-running processes that interact with dozens of external services in a single request: language model APIs, databases, file systems, sandboxed code interpreters, and third-party REST endpoints. Every one of these can fail — with network timeouts, rate-limit responses, malformed JSON, permission errors, or hardware faults. Without structured error handling, a single bad tool call propagates upward and aborts the entire agent turn, losing all intermediate progress and leaving resources in an inconsistent state.
Structured exception handling addresses three concrete problems:
— wrapping each tool invocation in its own
block means one failing tool produces a tool-error message the model can reason about, not a crash that terminates the whole run.
blocks ensure that semaphores are released, temporary directories are removed, database connections are returned to the pool, and progress is checkpointed even when something unexpected happens.
— catching specific exception subclasses lets the handler apply the right recovery strategy: retry a
, fall back to a secondary model on
Beyond correctness, structured handling is a debugging and observability tool. Logging inside the
handler — before converting the exception to a sanitized tool-error string — preserves the full traceback for engineers without leaking internal details to the model or the user.
— failures are caught close to where they occur, preventing cascading failures through the call stack.
guarantees cleanup code runs even when control leaves via
clauses let you apply different strategies (retry, fallback, abort, notify) to different failure classes.
attaches the original cause, preserving the full diagnostic chain without exposing it to end users.
— explicit exception handling makes error paths easy to test by raising specific exception types in mocks or fakes.
blocks reduce readability. The remedy is extracting inner operations into well-named functions, each with its own narrow exception handler.
is one of the most dangerous patterns in Python: it silently discards errors that should be investigated. Always log or re-raise.
discards any exception propagating out of the
block. This is almost never intentional.
— many Python developers do not know
exists, causing confusion during code review. Some teams avoid it for that reason.

— in Python, raising and catching an exception has measurable overhead (roughly 1–5 µs). For hot inner loops that check a condition thousands of times per second, a conditional check is faster. In I/O-bound agentic work the overhead is negligible.
instead of validating inputs up front makes code harder to reason about. Validate eagerly; catch defensively.

Calling external APIs, databases, or file systems where failures are expected and recoverable.
Executing agent tool calls that may raise well-defined error types the model should be told about.
Parsing structured data (JSON, YAML, XML) that may be malformed.
Translating low-level library exceptions into domain-specific error types.
Retrying transient errors (rate limits, network blips) with exponential back-off.
Releasing a lock, semaphore, or connection pool slot that was acquired before the try.
Deleting a temporary file or directory after processing.
Emitting a metric or span end-time regardless of outcome.

Do not wrap an entire agent loop in one mega-try. Catch only what you can handle.
as a lazy substitute for checking preconditions (LBYL — 'look before you leap' — is appropriate when the check is cheap and deterministic).
unless you genuinely need to clean up before exit and immediately re-raise.
The following walkthrough shows a production-grade pattern for a tool dispatcher in an agentic loop — the most common site where structured handling is needed.
Step 1: Define a specific exception hierarchy
Step 2: Write a narrow try/except per tool call
Step 3: Use finally for resource release
Step 4: Integrate into the agent loop

def run_agent_turn(messages: list, tool_calls: list) -> list:
    results = []
    for call in tool_calls:
        result = bounded_dispatch(call)  # never raises RecoverableToolError
        results.append(format_tool_message(call["id"], result))
    return results
    # FatalAgentError propagates up to the top-level request handler
Example 1 — basic retry with exponential back-off
Example 2 — file-backed checkpoint with atomic write
A production multi-tool agent turn dispatcher used at inference-serving scale combines per-call isolation, resource bounding, and progress checkpointing:

import asyncio
import logging
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Any

log = logging.getLogger(__name__)

@dataclass
class AgentTurnContext:
    run_id: str
    messages: list
    completed_results: list = field(default_factory=list)

async def execute_tool_call_async(call: dict) -> dict:
    """Simulate an async tool call that can fail."""
    await asyncio.sleep(0.01)   # I/O placeholder
    if call.get("name") == "bad_tool":
        raise RecoverableToolError("bad_tool is not available", "bad_tool")
    return {"output": f"result from {call['name']}"}

async def run_tool_calls(ctx: AgentTurnContext, tool_calls: list) -> None:
    """
    Execute tool calls concurrently; record each result (success or error)
    in ctx.completed_results so the model gets a complete picture.
    Checkpoint after each batch to survive container restarts.
    """
    sem = asyncio.Semaphore(8)   # cap concurrency

    async def bounded(call):
        async with sem:
            try:
                result = await execute_tool_call_async(call)
                return {"id": call["id"], "type": "tool_result", "content": result}
            except RecoverableToolError as exc:
                log.warning("run=%s tool=%s error=%s", ctx.run_id, call.get("name"), exc)
                return {"id": call["id"], "type": "tool_error",
                        "content": str(exc)}
            except Exception:
                log.exception("run=%s unexpected error in tool=%s",
                              ctx.run_id, call.get("name"))
                return {"id": call["id"], "type": "tool_error",
                        "content": "Unexpected internal error."}

    tasks = [asyncio.create_task(bounded(c)) for c in tool_calls]
    try:
        results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks)
        ctx.completed_results.extend(results)
    finally:
        # Checkpoint progress even if gather is cancelled mid-flight
        save_checkpoint(
            {"run_id": ctx.run_id, "results": ctx.completed_results},
            f"/tmp/agent_runs/{ctx.run_id}.json",
        )
        log.info("run=%s checkpointed %d results", ctx.run_id, len(ctx.completed_results))
This pattern provides three levels of protection: per-call
isolation prevents one tool failure from cancelling sibling tasks;
collects all results so the model can reason about the full outcome; and the outer
checkpoints progress so the run can be resumed after a container restart without re-executing already-completed tool calls.
Mistake 1 — bare except or except Exception: pass
Mistake 2 — cleanup only in except (skipped on success)

# BAD: lock is never released when call_api() succeeds
lock.acquire()
try:
    result = call_api()
except Exception:
    lock.release()   # only released on error!
    raise

# GOOD: finally guarantees release in all cases
lock.acquire()
try:
    result = call_api()
finally:
    lock.release()
Mistake 3 — return inside finally suppresses exceptions
Mistake 4 — catching too broad, too early
blocks small — ideally one logical operation per block. This makes it obvious which line raised and keeps handlers specific.
clauses from most specific to most general. Python checks them in order; a broad class listed first shadows all its subclasses.
) in handlers that convert or swallow exceptions. It automatically appends the full traceback to the log record.

Define a domain exception hierarchy and catch those instead of raw library exceptions at the agent layer. This insulates the rest of the code from library version changes.
so concurrent failures are collected rather than raising at the first one.
In CPython, entering and leaving a

block has negligible overhead — a few bytecode instructions that set up the exception table, which is consulted only when an exception actually occurs. The EAFP ('Easier to Ask Forgiveness than Permission') style is therefore efficient in Python for code paths where exceptions are rare.
Raising and catching an exception itself is more expensive: CPython must construct the exception object, unwind the stack, and evaluate each

clause. Benchmarks on CPython 3.12 show roughly 1–5 µs per raise+catch cycle in a tight loop. For I/O-bound agentic code where tool calls take 50–500 ms, this overhead is completely irrelevant. Only for tight numerical loops or high-throughput routing code should you prefer explicit conditionals over exceptions.
For scalability at the agent level, the key insight is that
blocks must complete quickly. A slow cleanup function (e.g., flushing a large buffer to disk) in
will delay every code path — success, failure, and cancellation. Move slow cleanup to background tasks or use a timeout:
or cancel-safe cleanup patterns. Never use
in async contexts — it is not safe with asyncio's event loop.
library provides declarative retry logic built on top of exception handling, removing the need to hand-roll back-off loops:
For one-liners where you want to ignore a specific exception,

from contextlib import suppress

# Instead of:
try:
    os.unlink(tmp_path)
except FileNotFoundError:
    pass

# Use:
with suppress(FileNotFoundError):
    os.unlink(tmp_path)
When managing a dynamic number of resources,
across N resources opened in a loop:

from contextlib import ExitStack

def process_many_files(paths):
    with ExitStack() as stack:
        handles = [stack.enter_context(open(p)) for p in paths]
        # All files are closed when the with block exits, even if one open() raised
        return [fh.read() for fh in handles]

import pytest

def test_unknown_tool_returns_error_message():
    ctx = make_test_context()
    result = safe_dispatch({"name": "nonexistent_tool", "id": "1"})
    assert result["type"] == "tool_error"
    assert "Unknown tool" in result["content"]

def test_fatal_error_propagates():
    with pytest.raises(FatalAgentError):
        safe_dispatch({"name": "fatal_tool", "id": "2"})
Several patterns address error handling; the right choice depends on the failure mode and recovery strategy required.
Verbose for simple checks; nesting hurts readability
Only works for resources supporting the protocol
Race-prone for file/DB checks; verbose for many conditions
Not idiomatic Python; lacks stack traces without extra work
Silencing one specific expected exception in one line
No logging; easy to accidentally hide real bugs
Adds dependency; overkill for one-off error handling
Exception handling is a security boundary in agentic systems. Two rules must always be followed:
Never expose raw tracebacks to end users or to the model's context window.
Tracebacks can reveal internal file paths, library versions, SQL queries, environment variable names, and other information useful to attackers. Convert to a sanitized user-facing message in the
handler; log the full exception server-side.
Never allow exception handlers to be used for authentication bypass.
raises for any reason (not just 'permission denied') the check is silently skipped. Always re-raise in permission-checking code.
immediately after any necessary cleanup. Swallowing them prevents the process from terminating cleanly and can cause container orchestrators to assume the process is healthy when it is stuck.

) in exception handlers so that run IDs, tool names, and exception types are machine-parseable fields — not buried in message strings — enabling automated alerting on high error rates for specific tools.
In production, monitor the ratio of
results per tool per time window. A spike indicates an upstream service degradation that your
Wrap each tool call or external API call in its own narrow
block so one failure does not abort an entire agent turn. Return a tool-error dict; let the model reason about it.
is the correct place for resource cleanup (releasing locks, closing connections, checkpointing progress). Cleanup in
alone is skipped on success paths.
Catch specific exception types and order clauses from most specific to most general. Never use bare
— always log and either recover or re-raise.
to translate exceptions across abstraction boundaries while preserving the full diagnostic chain in
, enabling per-exception-type recovery across parallel tool calls.
Never return or raise from inside
— it suppresses any in-flight exception. Only perform side-effects (cleanup, metrics, logging) in
Sanitize exceptions before surfacing them to end users or model prompts. Log full tracebacks server-side; send only short, informative strings through the tool-error channel.
for resources that support the context-manager protocol — they are harder to misuse and remove the risk of forgetting
